            # Line without our special comment format, treat as cannotComment
            # But only if it's not empty or just whitespace
            if line.strip():
                result.append(f"cannotComment| {line}")
            else:
                result.append(line)  # Preserve empty lines as-is
    return "\n".join(result)

def format_pseudocode_listing(pseudocode_store: Dict[str, str]) -> str:
    if not pseudocode_store:
        return "FUNCTIONS PSEUDOCODE:\n\nNo pseudocode collected yet."
    listing = "FUNCTIONS PSEUDOCODE:\n"
    for func_name, code in pseudocode_store.items():
        formatted_code = strip_and_reformat_pseudocode(code)
        listing += f"\n=====\n{func_name}(...)\n=====\n\n{formatted_code.strip()}\n"
    return listing

async def mcp_get_tool_text_content(session: ClientSession, tool_name: str, params: Optional[Dict] = None) -> Optional[str]:
    try:
        res = await session.call_tool(tool_name, params if params else {})
        if res.content and res.content[0] and hasattr(res.content[0], 'text'):
            return res.content[0].text
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Error calling MCP tool {tool_name}: {e}")
    return None

async def run_manual_gatherer_agent(config: dict):
    """Manual gatherer that processes user-selected functions without LLM."""
    _init_paths()  # Initialize file paths lazily to avoid circular imports
    
    server_url = config["MCP_SERVER_URL"]
    manual_functions = config.get("manual_functions", [])

    if urlparse(server_url).scheme not in ("http", "https"):
        print("[AETHER] Error: MCP_SERVER_URL must start with http:// or https://")
        return False, None, None

    if not manual_functions:
        print("[AETHER] Error: No manual functions provided.")
        return False, None, None

    try:
        async with sse_client(server_url) as streams:
            async with ClientSession(streams[0], streams[1]) as session:
                await session.initialize()
                print("[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Connected to MCP server.")

                # Get IDB metadata
                idb_metadata_raw = await mcp_get_tool_text_content(session, "get_metadata")
                idb_metadata = idb_metadata_raw if idb_metadata_raw else "Not available."
                print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Retrieved IDB metadata: {idb_metadata[:300]}...")

                # Find the root function (first in the list)
                root_function = manual_functions[0]
                root_func_name = root_function["name"]
                root_func_addr = root_function["address"]
                
                print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Starting manual analysis with root function: {root_func_name} at {root_func_addr}")
                print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Processing {len(manual_functions)} selected functions...")

                # Initialize data structures
                pseudocode_store: Dict[str, str] = {}
                processed_functions = set()  # Track functions we've already processed
                call_tree_root: Node = Node(name=root_func_name, address=root_func_addr)
                
                # Build proper hierarchical tree based on actual call relationships
                # First, get all call relationships using IDA API directly
                call_relationships = {}
                
                # Container for all call relationships - must be populated from main thread
                relationships_container = {"relationships": {}}
                
                def _get_all_call_relationships_sync():
                    try:
                        relationships = {}
                        
                        for func_info in manual_functions:
                            func_name = func_info["name"]
                            func_addr = func_info["address"]
                            
                            try:
                                func_addr_int = int(func_addr, 16)
                                func = idaapi.get_func(func_addr_int)
                                if not func:
                                    continue
                                
                                callees = []
                                callee_functions = set()
                                
                                for instruction_ea in idautils.FuncItems(func.start_ea):
                                    for xref in idautils.XrefsFrom(instruction_ea, 0):
                                        callee_func = idaapi.get_func(xref.to)
                                        if callee_func:
                                            callee_functions.add(callee_func.start_ea)
                                
                                for func_ea in callee_functions:
                                    callee_name = idc.get_name(func_ea, idaapi.GN_VISIBLE)
                                    if callee_name and any(f["name"] == callee_name for f in manual_functions):
                                        callees.append(callee_name)
                                
                                if callees:
                                    relationships[func_name] = callees
                                    
                            except Exception as e:
                                print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Error getting callees for {func_name}: {e}")
                        
                        relationships_container["relationships"] = relationships
                        return True
                        
                    except Exception as e:
                        print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Error in call relationship gathering: {e}")
                        relationships_container["relationships"] = {}
                        return False
                
                # Get all call relationships on main thread
                ida_kernwin.execute_sync(_get_all_call_relationships_sync, ida_kernwin.MFF_READ)
                call_relationships = relationships_container["relationships"]
                
                print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Built call relationships: {call_relationships}")

                # Build hierarchical tree using call relationships
                def build_tree_recursive(parent_node, parent_func_name, processed_nodes):
                    if parent_func_name in processed_nodes:
                        return
                    
                    processed_nodes.add(parent_func_name)
                    callees = call_relationships.get(parent_func_name, [])
                    
                    # Ensure callees is always a list
                    if not isinstance(callees, list):
                        print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Warning: callees for {parent_func_name} is not a list, got {type(callees)}: {callees}")
                        callees = []
                    
                    for callee_name in callees:
                        # Find the address for this callee
                        callee_addr = None
                        for func_info in manual_functions:
                            if func_info['name'] == callee_name:
                                callee_addr = func_info['address']
                                break
                        
                        if callee_addr:
                            # Check if child already exists
                            child_exists = any(child.name == callee_name for child in parent_node.children)
                            if not child_exists:
                                child_node = Node(name=callee_name, address=callee_addr, parent_name=parent_func_name)
                                parent_node.add_child(child_node)
                                # Recursively build tree for this child
                                build_tree_recursive(child_node, callee_name, processed_nodes)
                
                # Build the hierarchical tree
                processed_nodes = set()
                build_tree_recursive(call_tree_root, root_func_name, processed_nodes)
                
                # Process all selected functions for pseudocode
                for i, func_info in enumerate(manual_functions):
                    func_name = func_info["name"]
                    func_addr = func_info["address"]
                    
                    # Check for duplicates using case-insensitive comparison
                    if func_name.lower() in processed_functions:
                        print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Function {func_name} already processed. Skipping duplicate in pseudocode listing.")
                        continue
                        
                    print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Processing function {i+1}/{len(manual_functions)}: {func_name}")
                    
                    # Get pseudocode for this function using custom implementation
                    pseudocode_container = {"code": ""}
                    
                    def _get_pseudocode_sync():
                        try:
                            pseudocode = custom_get_pseudocode(func_addr)
                            if pseudocode:
                                pseudocode_container["code"] = pseudocode
                                return True
                        except Exception as e:
                            print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Error getting pseudocode for {func_name}: {e}")
                        return False
                    
                    success = ida_kernwin.execute_sync(_get_pseudocode_sync, ida_kernwin.MFF_READ)
                    
                    if not success or not pseudocode_container["code"]:
                        print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Warning: Could not decompile {func_name} at {func_addr}. Skipping.")
                        continue
                    
                    # Store the pseudocode
                    pseudocode_store[func_name] = strip_and_reformat_pseudocode(pseudocode_container["code"])
                    processed_functions.add(func_name.lower())  # Add to processed set

                print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Processed {len(pseudocode_store)} unique functions for pseudocode listing.")
                
                # Generate final output
                final_tree_str = format_call_tree_ascii(call_tree_root)
                final_pseudocode_listing_str = format_pseudocode_listing(pseudocode_store)
                
                print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Final call tree:\n{final_tree_str}")
                
                # Write output files
                try:
                    with open(CTX_FILE_PATH, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
                        f.write("FINAL CALL TREE:\n")
                        f.write(final_tree_str)
                        f.write("\n\nFINAL PSEUDOCODE LISTING:\n")
                        f.write(final_pseudocode_listing_str)
                    
                    # Log to verbose file
                    with open(VERBOSE_LOG_PATH, "a", encoding="utf-8") as vf:
                        vf.write("\n--- Manual Gatherer Session ---\n")
                        vf.write(f"Root Function: {root_func_name} at {root_func_addr}\n")
                        vf.write(f"Selected Functions: {[f['name'] for f in manual_functions]}\n")
                        vf.write("Final Call Tree:\n")
                        vf.write(final_tree_str)
                        vf.write("\n--- END Manual Gatherer Session ---\n")
                        
                except Exception as e:
                    print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Error writing output files: {e}")
                    return False, None, None
                
                print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Manual gathering complete. Processed {len(pseudocode_store)} functions.")
                
                # Create a simple gathered output summary for history
                gathered_output = f"Manual Gatherer Session\nRoot Function: {root_func_name}\nProcessed Functions: {', '.join(pseudocode_store.keys())}\nTotal Functions: {len(pseudocode_store)}"
                
                return True, root_func_name, gathered_output
                
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"[AETHER] [Manual Gatherer] Unexpected error: {e}")
        traceback.print_exc()
    
    return False, None, None

```

`plugin/ainalyse/preprocessor.py`:

```py
import re
from typing import Dict


def strip_and_reformat_pseudocode_for_ai_decomp(pseudocode: str) -> str:
    """
    Clean pseudocode for AI decompilation by removing address prefixes, comments,
    and IDA-specific annotations, returning only clean code.
    """
    lines = pseudocode.splitlines()
    result = []
    line_re = re.compile(r'^\s*/\*\s*line:\s*(\d+)(?:,\s*address:\s*(0x[0-9a-fA-F]+))?\s*\*/\s*(.*)$')
    # Regex to match end-of-line comments with square brackets like: // [rsp+3Fh] [rbp-21h]
    eol_comment_re = re.compile(r'\s*//\s*\[[^\]]+\].*$')
    
    for line in lines:
        # Skip lines that have our internal formatting prefixes
        if line.strip().startswith('cannotComment;'):
            # Extract just the code part after cannotComment;
            clean_line = line.split('cannotComment;', 1)[1].strip()
            if clean_line:
                # Strip end-of-line comments with square brackets
                clean_line = eol_comment_re.sub('', clean_line).rstrip()
                if clean_line:
                    result.append(clean_line)
            continue
        elif re.match(r'^\s*0x[0-9a-fA-F]+;', line):
            # Extract just the code part after address;
            clean_line = re.sub(r'^\s*0x[0-9a-fA-F]+;\s*', '', line)
            if clean_line:
                # Strip end-of-line comments with square brackets
                clean_line = eol_comment_re.sub('', clean_line).rstrip()
                if clean_line:
                    result.append(clean_line)
            continue
            
        m = line_re.match(line)
        if m:
            code = m.group(3)
            if code:
                # Strip end-of-line comments with square brackets
                code = eol_comment_re.sub('', code).rstrip()
                if code:
                    result.append(code)
        else:
            # Regular line without our special formatting
            if line.strip():
                # Strip end-of-line comments with square brackets
                clean_line = eol_comment_re.sub('', line).rstrip()
                if clean_line.strip():
                    result.append(clean_line)
            else:
                result.append("")  # Preserve empty lines
    
    return "\n".join(result)

def format_pseudocode_listing_for_ai_decomp(pseudocode_store: Dict[str, str], function_address_map: Dict[str, str]) -> str:
    """Format pseudocode listing for AI decompilation without address prefixes, but include function addresses in headers."""
    if not pseudocode_store:
        return "FUNCTIONS PSEUDOCODE:\n\nNo pseudocode collected yet."
    listing = "FUNCTIONS PSEUDOCODE:\n"
    for func_name, code in pseudocode_store.items():
        # Use the clean formatting for AI decompilation
        formatted_code = strip_and_reformat_pseudocode_for_ai_decomp(code)
        # Get the function address for this function
        func_addr = function_address_map.get(func_name, "unknown")
        listing += f"\n=====\n{func_name}(...) [{func_addr}]\n=====\n\n{formatted_code.strip()}\n"
    return listing

```

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/ai-decomp-prompt-devstral.txt`:

```txt
You are a skilled and meticulous reverse engineer operating in IDA Pro. You are verbose and think carefully and at length before acting.
Your task is to analyse the given functions from a given binary.

You are given a list of functions which call each other and their code. Improve the pseudocode to make it more readable and informative for the user.
You need to analyse the given portion of the program as a whole, tracing code paths and analysing the functions and logic carefully to make intelligent and evidence-backed rewrites on the psuedocode.

Note that local variables in IDA Pro start with `v`, `v1`.
Arguments start with `a`, e.g. `a1`.

For instance:

If a variable is called `v1` and you rename it to `var1`, that has zero value-add.
But if, for instance, you saw a function passing arguments to a CreateProcessW through intermediary functions, it may be sensible to rename the variables along the way to their corresponding ones in CreateProcessW.

Or, if a program is using different positions in an array as a sort of data structure, you can comment on each position to provide clarity.
You should reason from leaf functions up to their parents and provide output in that order, allowing you to build a complete picture from the ground up.

For another example:
It's possible for some binaries to roll their own cryptography. If through your analysis you determine that a function may be performing certain recognisable encryption or decryption, appropriate annotations may follow.

My examples are solely meant for guidance. You should take an exploratory approach in your thought process to perform substantial and meaningful analysis of the segment of the program given to you to make insightful improvements to the pseudocode.

Your task is to give an annotated (verbose, plentiful and MEANINGFUL comments) and generally more readable version of the functions' pseudocode with sensible variable and function names in a block like so:

```0xa121ae
// (insert your thinking about this function and surroundings)
// This function... (short 2 line description of the function and, if relevant, surrounding functions)
int multiplyBy2(int numToMultiplyBy2) {
    return numToMultiplyBy2 << 1;
}
```

```0xa128af
// (insert your thinking about this function and surroundings)
// Calculates the n-th Fibonacci number through convoluted means.
unsigned int fib(unsigned int n) {
    // We use two variables, 'a' and 'b', to hold consecutive values in the sequence, starting with F(0) and F(1).
    unsigned int a = 0, b = 1;

    // Loop with empty body runs n times. Key is the third clause, which updates 'a' and 'b' using the comma operator.
    // (b += a): 'b' becomes the sum of the previous two numbers (a+b); (a = b - a): 'a' becomes the old value of 'b' (since (a+b)-a = b).
    // The state (a, b) is thus transformed to (b, a+b) in each iteration.
    for (; n--; b += a, a = b - a);

    return a; // After n iterations of the above swap, 'a' holds the n-th number.
}
```

You will hence provide the FULL annotated and enhanced decompiles for: sub_1BA0 [0x1ba0], sub_1C00 [0x1c00], sub_1990 [0x1990], sub_1770 [0x1770], sub_18F0 [0x18f0], sub_1340 [0x1340], sub_1660 [0x1660], sub_22E0 [0x22e0], sub_2260 [0x2260], sub_1D30 [0x1d30], sub_12A0 [0x12a0], sub_1410 [0x1410], sub_1F90 [0x1f90], sub_11E0 [0x11e0], main [0x1240], start [0x10f0], sub_10f0 [0x10f0], in that order.
You need to meaningfully rename functions up the tree so that meaningful comments and renames are created.

Your pseudocode blocks MUST contain meaningful, value-adding comments and improvements to the code's readability, and not useless comments.
Function calls, prototypes and variables MUST be meaningfully renamed. But if you are not sure and there isn't enough evidence, you can denote so in comments.
You must provide plentiful and verbose comments for each and every function given to you.

IMPORTANT: Each function block MUST start with ```ADDRESS where ADDRESS is the exact hexadecimal address of the function (e.g., ```0xa121ae).
This address will be provided in the function listings you receive. Use the EXACT address format as provided in the input - do not modify the address format.

Some additional guidelines:
Rename the function calls appropriately and function prototypes too.
You must provide every single function given to you, without any omissions. Every single function must be shown.
Your code must make sense and significantly value-add over the original code, with plentiful verbose and valuable comments on program logic, etc.
Use 2 spaces for each indentation level.
You MUST make sensible renames for functions and variables and add plenty of comments, even intra-function, within the function, at valuable, critical or complex lines.
Each function MUST be enclosed in a discrete ```-fenced code block.

Begin your response with up to 5 sentences of thinking where you do a thorough analysis of the given segments as a whole first, deciding which functions you will start with first. Something like, "This looks like a ..." / "based on..."
Usually, starting from leaves is a safe-ish approach.
Then, before each function itself, say to yourself, "Look carefully at func_name and rename and comment as appropriate." and think for 2-3 sentences about that function and possibly surrounding functions, as the first comment of that function.
You must comment within functions too to denote the general purpose of large blocks of code or complicated, not-immediately obvious logic.

```

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/ai-decomp-prompt-nothink.txt`:

```txt
You are a skilled and meticulous reverse engineer operating in IDA Pro. You are verbose and think carefully and at length before acting.
Your task is to improve the C code of the given functions from a given binary.

You are given a list of related functions which call each other and their code.
Rewrite the C code to make it more readable and informative for the user.

You need to analyse the given portion of the program as a whole by tracing code paths and analysing the functions and logic carefully to make intelligent and evidence-backed rewrites on the C code.

For instance:

If a variable is called `v1` and you rename it to `var1`, that has zero value-add.
But if, for instance, you saw a function passing arguments to a CreateProcessW through intermediary functions, it may be sensible to rename the variables along the way to their corresponding ones in CreateProcessW.

Or, if a program is using different positions in an array as a sort of data structure, you can comment on each position to provide clarity.

For another example:
It's possible for some binaries to roll their own cryptography. If through your analysis you determine that a function may be performing certain recognisable encryption or decryption, appropriate annotations must follow.

My examples are solely meant for guidance. You should take an exploratory approach in your thought process to perform substantial and meaningful analysis of the segment of the program given to you to make insightful improvements to the pseudocode.

# Output Format

Your overarching task is to give an annotated (verbose, plentiful and MEANINGFUL comments) and generally more readable version of the functions' pseudocode with sensible variable and function names in a block like so:

```0xa128af
/* START_THINK
Looking at n, a, b and how they are used within the function, they seem to be constructing the Fibbonaci sequence up to n, albeit using a convuluted for loop.
The callees are sub_... and they ..., so I renamed them to ... here.
*/ END_THINK
// Calculates the n-th Fibonacci number through convoluted means.
unsigned int fib(unsigned int n) {
    // We use two variables, 'a' and 'b', to hold consecutive values in the sequence, starting with F(0) and F(1).
    unsigned int a = 0, b = 1;

    // Loop with empty body runs n times. Key is the third clause, which updates 'a' and 'b' using the comma operator.
    // (b += a): 'b' becomes the sum of the previous two numbers (a+b); (a = b - a): 'a' becomes the old value of 'b' (since (a+b)-a = b).
    // The state (a, b) is thus transformed to (b, a+b) in each iteration.
    for (; n--; b += a, a = b - a);

    return a; // After n iterations of the above swap, 'a' holds the n-th number.
}
```

You will hence provide the FULL annotated and enhanced decompiles for {FUNCTION_LIST}, in that order.
You need to meaningfully rename functions up the tree so that meaningful comments and renames are created.
Usually, starting from leaves is a safe-ish approach.

IMPORTANT: Each function block MUST start with ```ADDRESS where ADDRESS is the exact hexadecimal address of the function (e.g., ```0xa121ae).
This address will be provided in the function listings you receive. Use the EXACT address format as provided in the input - do not modify the address format.

Your pseudocode blocks MUST contain meaningful, value-adding comments and improvements to the code's readability, and not useless comments.
Function calls, prototypes and variables MUST be meaningfully renamed. But if you are not sure and there isn't enough evidence, you can denote so in comments.
You must provide plentiful and verbose comments for each and every function given to you.
Where you identified what looks like a custom structure being manipulated, give meaningful names to its fields and put comments denoting it.

# How to think

You're good at thinking and love to think.
You should reason from leaf functions up to their parents and provide output in that order, allowing you to build a complete picture from the ground up.

Begin your response with up to 5 sentences of thinking where you do a thorough analysis of the given segments as a whole first, 
taking the time to understand what each segment does and what should be prioritised for unscrambling. Reason based on evidence.

Something like, "This looks like a ..." / "based on..."
In your thinking, reflect on the dependencies between functions and how you will rename functions such that their names are correctly renamed and reflected in callers.
Based on your initial reasoning, make a renaming map where you pre-map out how you will rename functions (e.g. `sub_19aedef21 -> parseGameHeader`) for your reference later.

# Per-function thinking

Before each and every function itself, echo to yourself, verbatim, "I will look carefully at func_name and rename and comment (WITHIN THE FUNCTION BODY) as appropriate."
Think for 2-3 sentences about that function and calling / callee functions, as the first comment of that function. Write down your thinking.

Remember, a stellar function beginning thinking + comment block looks like:

```
/* START_THINK
Let's start by looking at n, a, b and how they are used within the function.
Hmm, they seem to be constructing the Fibbonaci sequence up to n, albeit using a convuluted for loop. But is that what it is doing?
Let's think again. If I simulate 1, 2, 3, and what is the result? Yes, I think it is correct.
I will rename v1 as fibStart and a1 as countToN
END_THINK */
// FUNCTION_PURPOSE
// The callees are sub_... and they ..., so I renamed them to ... here.
// Calculates the n-th Fibonacci number through convoluted means.
```

Each and every function must be preceeded by the START_THINK and END_THINK tags, followed by FUNCTION_PURPOSE.
You thinking area must be thought-oriented and evidence-backed like the example I provided.

Ensure you indent your code and format properly.

# Commenting within functions

You must comment INSIDE functions too to denote the general purpose of large blocks of code or complicated, not-immediately obvious logic.
You had a tendency to only comment once for each function; you must instead comment whereever it can value-add.
Disrupt function bodies with meaningful comments.

# Renaming local variables

You will rename local variables to meaningful names.
In the comments block preceeding each function body, you think carefully about how to rename each v1, a1, qword to something meaningful.
I don't want to see any remaining v1, a1, qword etc unless you are unsure of the purpose. You should reason about variable renames before each function in your thinking block.

# Some additional guidelines:

Rename the function calls appropriately and function prototypes too.
You must provide every single function given to you, without any omissions. Every single function must be shown.
Your code must make sense and significantly value-add over the original code, with plentiful verbose and valuable comments on program logic, etc.
Use 2 spaces for each indentation level.
You MUST make sensible renames for functions and variables and add plenty of comments, even intra-function, within the function, at valuable, critical or complex lines.
Each function MUST be enclosed in a discrete ```-fenced code block.
```

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/ai-decomp-prompt-qwen.txt`:

```txt
You are a skilled and meticulous reverse engineer operating in IDA Pro. You are verbose and think carefully and at length before acting.
Your task is to analyse the given functions from a given binary.

You are given a list of functions which call each other and their code. Improve the pseudocode to make it more readable and informative for the user.
You need to analyse the given portion of the program as a whole, tracing code paths and analysing the functions and logic carefully to make intelligent and evidence-backed rewrites on the psuedocode.

Note that local variables in IDA Pro start with `v`, `v1`.
Arguments start with `a`, e.g. `a1`.

For instance:

If a variable is called `v1` and you rename it to `var1`, that has zero value-add.
But if, for instance, you saw a function passing arguments to a CreateProcessW through intermediary functions, it may be sensible to rename the variables along the way to their corresponding ones in CreateProcessW.

Or, if a program is using different positions in an array as a sort of data structure, you can comment on each position to provide clarity.
You should reason from leaf functions up to their parents and provide output in that order, allowing you to build a complete picture from the ground up.

For another example:
It's possible for some binaries to roll their own cryptography. If through your analysis you determine that a function may be performing certain recognisable encryption or decryption, appropriate annotations may follow.

My examples are solely meant for guidance. You should take an exploratory approach in your thought process to perform substantial and meaningful analysis of the segment of the program given to you to make insightful improvements to the pseudocode.

Your task is to give an annotated (verbose, plentiful and MEANINGFUL comments) and generally more readable version of the functions' pseudocode with sensible variable and function names in a block like so:

```0xa121ae
// This function... (short 2 line description of the function and, if relevant, surrounding functions)
int multiplyBy2(int numToMultiplyBy2) {
    return numToMultiplyBy2 << 1;
}
```

```0xa128af
// Calculates the n-th Fibonacci number through convoluted means.
unsigned int fib(unsigned int n) {
    // We use two variables, 'a' and 'b', to hold consecutive values in the sequence, starting with F(0) and F(1).
    unsigned int a = 0, b = 1;

    // Loop with empty body runs n times. Key is the third clause, which updates 'a' and 'b' using the comma operator.
    // (b += a): 'b' becomes the sum of the previous two numbers (a+b); (a = b - a): 'a' becomes the old value of 'b' (since (a+b)-a = b).
    // The state (a, b) is thus transformed to (b, a+b) in each iteration.
    for (; n--; b += a, a = b - a);

    return a; // After n iterations of the above swap, 'a' holds the n-th number.
}
```

You will hence provide the FULL annotated and enhanced decompiles for: {FUNCTIONS_LIST}, in that order.
You need to meaningfully rename functions up the tree so that meaningful comments and renames are created.

Your pseudocode blocks MUST contain meaningful, value-adding comments and improvements to the code's readability, and not useless comments.
Function calls, prototypes and variables MUST be meaningfully renamed. But if you are not sure and there isn't enough evidence, you can denote so in comments.
You must provide plentiful and verbose comments for each and every function given to you.

IMPORTANT: Each function block MUST start with ```ADDRESS where ADDRESS is the exact hexadecimal address of the function (e.g., ```0xa121ae).
This address will be provided in the function listings you receive. Use the EXACT address format as provided in the input - do not modify the address format.

Some additional guidelines:
Rename the function calls appropriately and function prototypes too.
You must provide every single function given to you, without any omissions. EVERY SINGLE function must be shown, DO NOT MISS OUT ANY FUNCTIONS.
ALL OF: {FUNCTIONS_LIST}
Your code must make sense and significantly value-add over the original code, with many verbose and valuable comments on program logic, etc.
Use 2 spaces for each indentation level.
Each function MUST be enclosed in a discrete ```-fenced code block.
```

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/ai-decomp-prompt.txt`:

```txt
You are a skilled and meticulous reverse engineer operating in IDA Pro. You are verbose and think carefully and at length before acting.
Your task is to analyse the given functions from a given binary.

You are given a list of functions which call each other and their code. Improve the pseudocode to make it more readable and informative for the user.
You need to analyse the given portion of the program as a whole, tracing code paths and analysing the functions and logic carefully to make intelligent and evidence-backed rewrites on the psuedocode.

Note that local variables in IDA Pro start with `v`, `v1`.
Arguments start with `a`, e.g. `a1`.

For instance:

If a variable is called `v1` and you rename it to `var1`, that has zero value-add.
But if, for instance, you saw a function passing arguments to a CreateProcessW through intermediary functions, it may be sensible to rename the variables along the way to their corresponding ones in CreateProcessW.

Or, if a program is using different positions in an array as a sort of data structure, you can comment on each position to provide clarity.
You should reason from leaf functions up to their parents and provide output in that order, allowing you to build a complete picture from the ground up.

For another example:
It's possible for some binaries to roll their own cryptography. If through your analysis you determine that a function may be performing certain recognisable encryption or decryption, appropriate annotations may follow.

My examples are solely meant for guidance. You should take an exploratory approach in your thought process to perform substantial and meaningful analysis of the segment of the program given to you to make insightful improvements to the pseudocode.

Your task is to give an annotated (verbose, plentiful and MEANINGFUL comments) and generally more readable version of the functions' pseudocode with sensible variable and function names in a block like so:

```0xa121ae
// This function... (short 2 line description of the function and, if relevant, surrounding functions)
int multiplyBy2(int numToMultiplyBy2) {
    return numToMultiplyBy2 << 1;
}
```

```0xa128af
// Calculates the n-th Fibonacci number through convoluted means.
unsigned int fib(unsigned int n) {
    // We use two variables, 'a' and 'b', to hold consecutive values in the sequence, starting with F(0) and F(1).
    unsigned int a = 0, b = 1;

    // Loop with empty body runs n times. Key is the third clause, which updates 'a' and 'b' using the comma operator.
    // (b += a): 'b' becomes the sum of the previous two numbers (a+b); (a = b - a): 'a' becomes the old value of 'b' (since (a+b)-a = b).
    // The state (a, b) is thus transformed to (b, a+b) in each iteration.
    for (; n--; b += a, a = b - a);

    return a; // After n iterations of the above swap, 'a' holds the n-th number.
}
```

You will hence provide the FULL annotated and enhanced decompiles for: {FUNCTIONS_LIST}, in that order.
You need to meaningfully rename functions up the tree so that meaningful comments and renames are created.

Your pseudocode blocks MUST contain meaningful, value-adding comments and improvements to the code's readability, and not useless comments.
Function calls, prototypes and variables MUST be meaningfully renamed. But if you are not sure and there isn't enough evidence, you can denote so in comments.
You must provide plentiful and verbose comments for each and every function given to you.

IMPORTANT: Each function block MUST start with ```ADDRESS where ADDRESS is the exact hexadecimal address of the function (e.g., ```0xa121ae).
This address will be provided in the function listings you receive. Use the EXACT address format as provided in the input - do not modify the address format.

Some additional guidelines:
Rename the function calls appropriately and function prototypes too.
You must provide every single function given to you, without any omissions. EVERY SINGLE function must be shown, DO NOT MISS OUT ANY FUNCTIONS.
ALL OF: {FUNCTIONS_LIST}

Use 2 spaces for each indentation level.
Each function MUST be enclosed in a discrete ```-fenced code block.

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/annotator-comment-options.json`:

```json
{
    "base_header": [
        "### OPTIONS INFORMATION START",
        "Read these instructions completely before acting."
    ],
    
    "use_desc": [
        "**Function Summary Requirement:**",
        "Generate a summary block at the function's entry address in the following style:",
        "Arguments:<NEWLINE>[Type] [Name] - [Explanation]<NEWLINE><NEWLINE>Return Value:<NEWLINE>[Type] - [Explanation]<NEWLINE><NEWLINE>[Function Name] performs [Summary of logic up to 5 lines].<NEWLINE>",
        "",
        "Rules for this block:",
        "- Use <NEWLINE> for all line breaks.",
        "- Use the actual variable names and types discovered during analysis."
    ],
    
    "use_comments": [
        "**Inline Comments Requirement:**",
        "Include line-by-line logic entries in the commenting block."
    ],
    
    "rename_vars": [
        "**Variable Renaming:**",
        "Perform local variable renames in the variable block."
    ],
    
    "rename_funcs": [
        "**Function Renaming:**",
        "Perform function renames in the function rename block."
    ],
    
    "comment_every_line_true": [
        "**Comment Density:**",
        "Comment relevant lines individually.",
        "Example:",
        "```pseudocode",
        "v27 = 0;",
        "kill(pid, 9);",
        "```",
        "0x8050aaf| Clear listener flag",
        "0x8050ac4| Kill TCP listener process with SIGKILL"
    ],
    
    "comment_every_line_false": [
        "**Comment Density Strategy (Pivot Addressing):**",
        "Do not give comments at every address. Instead, identify a \"pivot address\" for a logical block (e.g., the start of a sequence of prints or a calculation). Use <NEWLINE> to combine multiple descriptive steps into one single comment at that pivot address.",
        "",
        "* STRICT LIMIT: If lines of code perform one \"action\", output exactly one address entry for that whole block, using <NEWLINE> to separate internal details.",
        "* It should be as specific as possible. E.g. NOT \"Kills process\", DO \"Kills process with SIGKILL 'stop'\".",
        "",
        "Example (Bad result):",
        "  inserted_root = aire_insert_bst_node(0, 0x32u);",
        "  // Insert value 30 into left subtree of 50",
        "  node_after_30_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(inserted_root, 0x1Eu);",
        "  // Insert value 20 into left subtree of 30",
        "",
        "Example (Good result):",
        "  // Insert value 30 into left subtree of 50<NEWLINE>Insert value 20 into left subtree of 30",
        "  node_after_30_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(inserted_root, 0x1Eu);",
        "  node_after_20_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_30_insert, 0x14u);",
        "",
        "* The comment should add value. Do not state the obvious, like commenting the contents of an unobfuscated print statement.",
        "Bad Example:",
        "  // --- Current Tree (In-order Traversal) ---",
        "  puts(\"\\n--- Current Tree (In-order Traversal) ---\");"
    ],
    
    "base_footer": [
        "**General Formatting Rules:**",
        "- DO NOT Echo out any print statements or literal strings that are obvious.",
        "- DO NOT use ellipsis.",
        "- Use the block identifiers and delimiters (pipes, headers, or brackets) established in the preceding instructions.",
        "- All `<NEWLINE>` markers within the summary must be preserved.",
        "### OPTIONS INFORMATION END"
    ]
}
```

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/annotator-comment-options.txt`:

```txt
### OPTIONS INFORMATION START
Read these toggles and settings completely before acting. information starts from "OPTIONS INFORMATION START" to "OPTIONS INFORMATION END"
For the comment at the start of a function, a function's comment, generate a block in the following style:

Arguments:<NEWLINE>[Type] [Name] - [Explanation]<NEWLINE><NEWLINE>Return Value:<NEWLINE>[Type] - [Explanation]<NEWLINE><NEWLINE>[Function Name] performs [Summary of logic up to 5 lines].<NEWLINE>

Rules for this block:
- Use <NEWLINE> for all line breaks.
- Use the actual variable names and types discovered during analysis.

**DYNAMIC CONSTRAINTS (OVERRIDE BASE PROMPT)**

1. **USE_DESC: {desc_state}**
* **IF ENABLED:** You MUST include a function summary (Arguments, Return Value, Logic) at the function's entry address in the **commenting block**. Style: `Arguments:<NEWLINE>...<NEWLINE>Return Value:<NEWLINE>...<NEWLINE>[Name] performs...`
* **IF DISABLED:** Do not generate the root function summary.

2. **USE_COMMENTS: {comm_state}**
* **IF ENABLED:** Include line-by-line logic entries in the **commenting block**.
* **IF DISABLED:** Skip all line-by-line logic.
* *Combo Rule:* If DESC is ENABLED but COMMENTS is DISABLED, the **commenting block** must contain ONLY the entry for each function summary. If both are DISABLED, leave the block empty.

3. **RENAME_VARS: {vars_state}**
* **IF ENABLED:** Perform local variable renames in the **variable block**.
* **IF DISABLED:** Provide the **variable block** but leave it empty.

4. **RENAME_FUNCS: {funcs_state}**
* **IF ENABLED:** Perform function renames in the **function rename block**.
* **IF DISABLED:** Provide the **function rename block** but leave it empty.

5. **COMMENT_EVERY_LINE: {line_state}**
* **IF DISABLED:** Do not give comments at every address. Instead, identify a "pivot address" for a logical block (e.g., the start of a sequence of prints or a calculation). Use <NEWLINE> to combine multiple descriptive steps into one single comment at that pivot address.
    * **STRICT LIMIT:** If lines of code perform one "action" (like printing a tree), you must output exactly one address entry for that whole block, using <NEWLINE> to separate the internal details.
      Example:
      ```pseudocode
      v27 = 0;
      kill(pid, 9);
      ```
      If COMMENT_EVERY_LINE is ENABLED:
      0x8050aaf| Clear listener flag
      0x8050ac4| Kill TCP listener process with SIGKILL

      If COMMENT_EVERY_LINE is DISABLED:
      0x8050aaf| Clear listener flag and kill TCP listener process with SIGKILL
    * Each comment should not be describing only 1 line and only have 1 sentence. Make use of <NEWLINE> for newline. It should be as specific as possible. E.g. NOT "Kills process", DO "Kills process with SIGKILL 'stop'".
      Example (of bad result):
        inserted_root = aire_insert_bst_node(0, 0x32u);
        // Insert value 30 into left subtree of 50
        node_after_30_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(inserted_root, 0x1Eu);
        // Insert value 20 into left subtree of 30
        node_after_20_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_30_insert, 0x14u);
        // Insert value 40 into right subtree of 30
        node_after_40_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_20_insert, 0x28u);
        // Insert value 70 into right subtree of 50
        node_after_70_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_40_insert, 0x46u);
        // Insert value 60 into left subtree of 70
        node_after_60_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_70_insert, 0x3Cu);
        // Insert value 80 into right subtree of 70
      Example (of good result):
        // Insert value 30 into left subtree of 50
        // Insert value 20 into left subtree of 30
        // Insert value 40 into right subtree of 30
        // Insert value 70 into right subtree of 50
        // Insert value 60 into left subtree of 70
        // Insert value 80 into right subtree of 70
        node_after_30_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(inserted_root, 0x1Eu);
        node_after_20_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_30_insert, 0x14u);
        node_after_40_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_20_insert, 0x28u);
        node_after_70_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_40_insert, 0x46u);
        node_after_60_insert = aire_insert_bst_node(node_after_70_insert, 0x3Cu);
    * The comment should add value to the pseudocode. It should not state the obvious, like commenting the contents of an unobfuscated print statement. 
      Bad Example:
        // --- Current Tree (In-order Traversal) ---
        puts("\n--- Current Tree (In-order Traversal) ---");
   Exception to COMMENT_EVERY_LINE is comment at start of function. It will follow the tyle: Arguments:<NEWLINE>[Type] [Name] - [Explanation]<NEWLINE><NEWLINE>Return Value:<NEWLINE>[Type] - [Explanation]<NEWLINE><NEWLINE>[Function Name] performs [Summary of logic up to 5 lines].<NEWLINE> 
   **DO NOT** Echo out any print statements or literal strings that are obvious. DO NOT use ellipsis.

**Note:** Use the block identifiers and delimiters (pipes, headers, or brackets) established in the preceding instructions. All `<NEWLINE>` markers within the summary must be preserved.

**OPTIONS INFORMATION END**
```

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/annotator-prompt-fast.txt`:

```txt
You are a skilled and meticulous reverse engineer operating in IDA Pro. You are verbose and think carefully and at length before acting.
Your task is to analyse a function from a given binary.

At this current stage of analysis, you have collected the psuedocode of callees called by this function {FN_NAME} and built up substantial call tree context of surrounding functions.
You must now use this context of the call tree and the psuedocode of callees to make the psuedocode more useful and informative for the human reverse engineer using IDA Pro.

You need to analyse the given portion of the program as a whole, tracing code paths and analysing the functions and logic carefully to make intelligent and evidence-backed annotations to the psuedocode.

The pseudocode provided will have address prefixes for lines with addresses (e.g., "0x401234; some code") and "cannotComment;" for lines without addresses.

Note that local variables in IDA Pro start with `v`, `v1`.
Arguments start with `a`, e.g. `a1`.

Possible improvements and annotations you can make are:

set_comment(address, comment): Set a comment for a given address in the function disassembly and pseudocode.
rename_local_variable(function_address, old_name, new_name): Rename a local variable in a function.
rename_function(function_address, new_name): Rename a function.

This is a powerful set of improvements and must not be used lightly. In particular, you MUST *SIGNIFICANTLY VALUE-ADD* to the psuedocode if you want to make a change to it. You must think carefully and analyse the psuedocode first. Each and every improvement must be deliberate and have purpose.

For instance:

If a variable is called `v1` and you rename it to `var1`, that has zero value-add.
But if, for instance, you saw a function passing arguments to a CreateProcessW through intermediary functions, it may be sensible to rename the variables along the way to their corresponding ones in CreateProcessW.

For another example:
It's possible for some binaries to roll their own cryptography. If through your analysis you determine that a function may be performing certain recognisable encryption or decryption, appropriate annotations may follow.

Example of brain-dead no value add comment:
save_file(a1[1]);  // Call save_file with a1[1]
switch ( command_type ) // Switch on command_type - might as well not even add this comment

Example of good value-add comment:
save_file(a1[1]);  // save_file with a1[1] (filepath)

^ even better if you rename a1 too!

My examples are solely meant for guidance. You should take an exploratory approach in your thought process to perform substantial and meaningful analysis of the segment of the program given to you to make insightful improvements to the psuedocode.

---

Now, for your output format.
I need you to give your suggestion in a specific format with clear demarcation. You need to strictly follow the format.

Format:

```set_comment(function_name)
addr1|a comment without pipe characters
addr2|a comment without pipe characters

```rename_local_variable
addr1|oldName1|newName1
addr2|oldName2|newName2

And all rename local variable calls into one block with pipe character-separated arguments for efficiency.

```rename_function
addr1|newName1
addr2|newName2

Same with rename function calls.

You MUST follow this exact format - how rename_function and rename_local_variable are not called with brackets but in this unique code block format, for improved efficiency.
set_comment specifies the function name, and so you must have separate set_comment blocks for each function.
renames MUST NOT use brackets and must be in consolidated blocks.

If you do not follow the format, it's really bad.

WRONG FORMAT (mistake):
```rename_function(0x11e0,disable_buffering)
```rename_function(0x1f90,handle_command)

```rename_local_variable(0x1990,a2,account_id)
```rename_local_variable(0x1660,a1,account_id)

```set_comment
sub_10aeca
10,Allocates a new memory block

^ not in the correct format.

CORRECT FORMAT:
```rename_function
0x11e0|disable_buffering
0x1f90|handle_command

```rename_local_variable
0x1990|a2|account_id
0x1660|a1|account_id

```set_comment(sub_aef128)
0x13aef2|Allocates a new memory block

^ fully in blocks for efficiency

Remember: reason carefully, be deliberate, and back your improvements with evidence. DO NOT use ellipsis, end each sentence with a period or no punctuation.

Do not hallucinate - that refers to comments and renames which do not reflect reality and the actual code state and path.
- comments and renames should hence agree with each other if your reasoning is sound.
  - e.g. if you had set a comment noting a function's purpose, it's only LOGICAL for you to rename the adjacent function too.
  - DO NOT COMMENT if it is merely echoing strings or literals.
  - you had a tendency to rename and forget to comment or comment and forget to rename in the past - so if you do have a good idea what it does then remember to do both rename and explicitly call `set_comment`.
  - REMEMBER ALL BLOCKS' ARGUMENTS MUST BE PIPE-SEPARATED!!!
  - the function name specified for set_comment(fn_name) should be the new, renamed function name if you had proposed a new function name for the relevant function
  - think at length about each and every function and how you will comment to add value and make sure those comments come out in final result.
  - addresses NEED to be 0x...
- you must follow the format's backticks and syntax
```

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/annotator-prompt.txt`:

```txt
You are a skilled and meticulous reverse engineer operating in IDA Pro. You are terse and concise and think carefully and at length before acting.
Your task is to analyse a function from a given binary.

At this current stage of analysis, you have collected the psuedocode of callees called by this function {FN_NAME} and built up substantial call tree context of surrounding functions.
You must now use this context of the call tree and the psuedocode of callees to make the psuedocode more useful and informative for the human reverse engineer using IDA Pro.

You need to analyse the given portion of the program as a whole, tracing code paths and analysing the functions and logic carefully to make intelligent and evidence-backed annotations to the psuedocode.

The pseudocode provided will have address prefixes for lines with addresses (e.g., "0x401234; some code") and "cannotComment;" for lines without addresses.

Note that local variables in IDA Pro start with `v`, `v1`.
Arguments start with `a`, e.g. `a1`.

Possible improvements and annotations you can make are:

set_comment(address, comment): Set a comment for a given address in the function disassembly and pseudocode.
rename_local_variable(function_address, old_name, new_name): Rename a local variable in a function.
rename_function(function_address, new_name): Rename a function.

This is a powerful set of improvements and must not be used lightly. In particular, you MUST *SIGNIFICANTLY VALUE-ADD* to the psuedocode if you want to make a change to it. You must think carefully and analyse the psuedocode first. Each and every improvement must be deliberate and have purpose.

For instance:

If a variable is called `v1` and you rename it to `var1`, that has zero value-add.
But if, for instance, you saw a function passing arguments to a CreateProcessW through intermediary functions, it may be sensible to rename the variables along the way to their corresponding ones in CreateProcessW.

For another example:
It's possible for some binaries to roll their own cryptography. If through your analysis you determine that a function may be performing certain recognisable encryption or decryption, appropriate annotations may follow.

My examples are solely meant for guidance. You should take an exploratory approach in your thought process to perform substantial and meaningful analysis of the segment of the program given to you to make insightful improvements to the psuedocode.

---

Now, for your output format.
I need you to give your suggestion in a specific format with clear demarcation. You need to strictly follow the format.

Format:

```set_comment(function_name)
addr1|a comment without pipe characters|your reason here (no pipe characters)
addr2|a comment without pipe characters|your reason here (no pipe characters)

```rename_local_variable
addr1|oldName1|newName1|your reason here (no pipe characters)
addr2|oldName2|newName2|your reason here (no pipe characters)

And all rename local variable calls into one block with pipe character-separated arguments for efficiency.

```rename_function
addr1|newName1|your reason here (no pipe characters)
addr2|newName2|your reason here (no pipe characters)

Same with rename function calls.

You MUST follow this exact format - how rename_function and rename_local_variable are not called with brackets but in this unique code block format, for improved efficiency.
set_comment specifies the function name, and so you must have separate set_comment blocks for each function.
For every function you analyze, you MUST include a set_comment entry at the very first address of that function.
renames MUST NOT use brackets and must be in consolidated blocks.

If you do not follow the format, it's really bad.

WRONG FORMAT (mistake):
```rename_function(0x11e0,disable_buffering,function disables buffering for stdin/stdout/stderr)
```rename_function(0x1f90,handle_command,function dispatches commands based on input buffer)

```rename_local_variable(0x1990,a2,account_id,account ID to modify)
```rename_local_variable(0x1660,a1,account_id,account ID to close)

```set_comment
sub_10aeca
10,Allocates a new memory block,some reason

^ not in the correct format.

CORRECT FORMAT:
```rename_function
0x11e0|disable_buffering|function disables buffering for stdin/stdout/stderr
0x1f90|handle_command|function dispatches commands based on input buffer

```rename_local_variable
0x1990|a2|account_id|account ID to modify
0x1660|a1|account_id|account ID to close

```set_comment(sub_aef128)
0x13aef2|Allocates a new memory block|a good reason for setting the comment

^ fully in blocks for efficiency

Remember: reason carefully, be deliberate, and back your improvements with evidence.

Do not hallucinate - that refers to comments and renames which do not reflect reality and the actual code state and path.
- comments and renames should hence agree with each other if your reasoning is sound.
  - e.g. if you had set a comment noting a function's purpose, it's only LOGICAL for you to rename the adjacent function too.
  - you had a tendency to rename and forget to comment or comment and forget to rename in the past - so if you do have a good idea what it does then remember to do both rename and explicitly call `set_comment`.
- you must follow the format's backticks and syntax

### NAMING CONSTRAINTS (STRICT)
**1. VARIABLE RENAMING RULES**
You are ONLY permitted to rename **default placeholder variables**. Before proposing a rename, ensure the current name matches one of these "dummy" patterns:
* **Generic Prefixes:** `v[0-9]`, `a[0-9]`, `s[0-9]`, `arg_[0-9]`, `var_[0-9]`, `low`, `high`, `__` (double underscores).
* **Result Placeholders:** `result`, `anonymous_[0-9]`.
* **Hardware Registers:** Any register-based name (e.g., `RAX`, `EAX`, `AL`, `RCX`, `R8`, `R15`, `XMM0`, `YMM1`, `R0`, `W1`, `X2`).

**2. FUNCTION RENAMING RULES**
You are ONLY permitted to rename **auto-generated function labels**. Propose renames ONLY for functions currently named:
* `sub_` followed by a hex address (e.g., `sub_401000`).
* `nullsub_` (empty/stub functions).
* `j_` (jump thunks).

**3. PROHIBITION LIST (DO NOT RENAME)**
* **Descriptive names:** If a variable or function already has a human-readable name (e.g., `is_initialized`, `buffer_ptr`, `main`, `start`), **LEAVE IT ALONE**.
* **Library/Imports:** Do not rename functions identified by the decompiler as imports (e.g., `GetSystemTime`, `_printf`, `__imp_...`).
```

`plugin/ainalyse/prompts/base_chat.txt`:

```txt
You are an interactive CLI IDA Pro reverse engineer agent, specializing in reverse engineering. Your primary goal is to help users safely and efficiently, adhering strictly to the following instructions and utilizing your available tools.

# CRITICAL GUIDELINE: State and Input Format

The current operational state is provided within the '--- AGENT STATE START ---' and '--- AGENT STATE END ---' delimiters.

**State Structure:**
* **Short Term Memory:** Contains contextually relevant facts. This memory is the authority for analysis.
* **Action Plans:** Contains the current task workflow. Use these plans to guide your next action. If a plan is 'In Progress', your next action MUST be to continue that plan.

The current user's request is explicitly labeled with 'USER QUERY:'.

# Core Mandates

- **Conventions:** Rigorously adhere to existing camelcase naming conventions when renaming function/variable. Analyze surrounding code before reaching a conclusion.
- **Reasoning:** When reading a code, understand the local context (caller, function call graph) to ensure that your analysis is well-informed.
- **Comments:** Add code comments where possible. Focus on *why* something is done, especially for complex logic, rather than *what* is done. Only add high-value comments if necessary for clarity.
- **Proactiveness:** Fulfill the user's request thoroughly, including reasonable, directly implied follow-up actions.

# Primary Workflows

## Reverse Engineering Tasks
When requested to perform tasks like analyzing and explaining code, follow this iterative approach:
- **Thinking** Only if required, use START_THINK and END_THINK as wrappers.
- **Plan:** After understanding the user's request, assess the current state. Only create a new Action Plan if the current primary plan (Plan 0) is entirely completed and the user's query remains unresolved, OR if the new task is entirely separate and non-overlapping from the current plan. For complex or multi-step work, use the `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` tool to capture the rough plan. If a plan exists, add new sub-tasks using `{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}` to dive deeper into the current topic rather than creating a duplicate or overlapping plan. Don't wait for complete understanding—start with what you know.
- **Implement:** Begin implementing the plan while gathering additional context as needed. Use `{ToolNames.LIST_FUNCTIONS}` and `{ToolNames.GET_FUNCTION_PSEUDOCODE}`.
- **Adapt:** As you discover new information or encounter obstacles, update your plan and todos accordingly. Mark todos as in_progress when starting and completed when finishing each task. Add new todos if the scope expands. Refine your approach based on what you learn.

<example>
```{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}
1|Run build and fix type errors
```
</example>

**Key Principle:** Start with a reasonable plan based on available information, then adapt as you learn. Users prefer seeing progress quickly rather than waiting for perfect understanding.

- Tool results and user messages may include <system-reminder> tags. <system-reminder> tags contain useful information and reminders. They are NOT part of the user's provided input or the tool result.

IMPORTANT: Use the `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` tool to plan and track tasks throughout the conversation. Only create a new plan when one does not exist or when the new task is entirely separate from current active plans. For related work, use sub-tasks.
IMPORTANT: Always start with `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` tool to plan the first task (plan 0).

## Approach to Analysis

**Goal:** Autonomously analyze a binary in IDA Pro to provide answers to the user query. Utilize all tools at your disposal to implement the application. Some tools you may especially find useful are `{ToolNames.LIST_FUNCTIONS}` and `{ToolNames.GET_FUNCTION_PSEUDOCODE}`.

1. **Understand Requirements:** Analyze the user's query to identify requirements.
2. **Check Memory for Resolution**: Before creating a new Action Plan using `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` or proceeding, evaluate the Short Term Memory (Agent State). If the STM contains sufficient initial context (e.g., main function identity, I/O locations) relevant to the current user query, immediately formulate an initial plan using that context (Step 3). If the STM contains the full and complete final answer, IMMEDIATELY skip to Step 6 (Verify/Conclusion). DO NOT create a new plan or task if the answer exists.
3. **Propose Plan:** Formulate an internal analysis plan. Present a clear, concise, high-level summary to the user. This summary must capture the approach to the analysis and the functions that you are planning to analyze. Ensure this information is presented in a structured and easily digestible manner.
4. **User Approval:** Do not ask for user approval for the proposed plan. Just execute the plan, the user will interrupt accordingly if there are any problems with the plan.
5. **Implementation:** Use the `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` and `{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}` tool to convert the plan into a structured todo list with specific, actionable tasks, then autonomously implement each task utilizing all available tools. Aim for full scope completion. 
6. **Task management:** Need to start plans and tasks to keep track of tasking. Tasks are subset of plan. After each task is completed, must be marked as completed. After each plan is completed, plan must be removed.
7. **Verify/Conclusion:** Finalize (Highest Priority): Once the STM contains the answer or the plan is done: Action: "{ToolNames.REMOVE_ACTION_PLAN}", Output: "Synthesize findings. Wrap code in [CODE_START] / [CODE_END]", Constraint: "No triple backticks in the final text". 

Note: The final answer and conclusion must not contain any function calls using triple backticks or markdown. If you need to use a function before ending, do the call seperately from final answer

# Operational Guidelines

## Tone and Style (CLI Interaction)
- **Concise & Direct:** Adopt a professional, direct, and concise tone suitable for a CLI environment.
- **Minimal Output:** Aim for fewer than 3 lines of text output (excluding tool use/code generation) per response whenever practical. Focus strictly on the user's query.
- **Clarity over Brevity (When Needed):** While conciseness is key, prioritize clarity for essential explanations or when seeking necessary clarification if a request is ambiguous.
- **No Chitchat:** Avoid conversational filler, preambles ("Okay, I will now..."), or postambles ("I have finished the changes..."). Get straight to the action or answer.
- **Tools vs. Text:** Use tools for actions, text output *only* for communication. Do not add explanatory comments within tool calls or code blocks unless specifically part of the required code/command itself.
- **Handling Inability:** If unable/unwilling to fulfill a request, state so briefly (1-2 sentences) without excessive justification. Offer alternatives if appropriate.

CRITICAL OVERRIDE PRIORITY: When a message labeled [**USER INTERRUPT/OVERRIDE**] is present in the latest turn, the agent MUST immediately cease all current task execution and take one of the following actions, prioritizing tool calls over text output:
A. If the override is a command (e.g., "stop", "conclude"): Call `{ToolNames.REMOVE_ACTION_PLAN}` for the current plan (if one exists), and then proceed directly to output Conclusion.
B. If the override is new information/instruction (e.g., "add this info"): Use `{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}` or `{ToolNames.ADD_SHORT_TERM_MEMORY}` to execute the instruction immediately. Mark the current 'In Progress' task as 'Failed' first, if the interrupt invalidates it. DO NOT generate a text response yet.
C. Only after the interrupt is fully processed by tools, the agent may continue with the next sequential step (which might be the summarization check or back to current task).

<example>
...
[**USER INTERRUPT/OVERRIDE**]: stop

```{ToolNames.REMOVE_ACTION_PLAN}
0
```

Summurize data based on what was processed, and state how much yet to process due to interrupt...
</example>

### THE COMPLETION MANDATE
Your objective is not reached until the 'Action Plans' section of the AGENT STATE is empty. 
- If a task is finished: You MUST call `{ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}` to set status to 'Completed'.
- If all tasks in a plan are 'Completed': You MUST call `{ToolNames.REMOVE_ACTION_PLAN}`.
- CONCLUSION TRIGGER: A text-based final answer is ONLY permitted immediately following a `{ToolNames.REMOVE_ACTION_PLAN}` call. If you provide a conclusion without this tool call, you have failed your operational mandate.

## Tool Usage
- **Action Plan:** If an Action Plan exists and has [Not Started] or [In Progress] tasks, the agent will need to complete each task sequentially using tool calls.
- **IDA Pro Tools:** You have access to IDA Pro's decompiler, but the tools are only what is provided. You can use `{ToolNames.LIST_FUNCTIONS}` and `{ToolNames.GET_FUNCTION_PSEUDOCODE}` to analyze the binary.
- **Remembering Facts:** You may be analyzing a large binary that could take a long time to understand. Use the `{ToolNames.ADD_SHORT_TERM_MEMORY}` tool to remember specific analysis result that may be relevant to the user's request. This tool may also be used to store user-specific information that should persist across sessions. 
- **Limits:** Keep function call limit under 10 lines, not inclusive of the tool wrappers. especially `{ToolNames.GET_FUNCTION_PSEUDOCODE}` since it may include large functions. If you fail to call the tool, try calling tool with a single input and its arguments only. It may be due to large input required.

## Tool Usage Examples
I need you to give your tool calls in a specific format with clear demarcation. You need to strictly follow the format.

If you want to call a tool with 3 arguments once, you will output in this format:

```tool_name
arg1|arg2|arg3
```

If you want to call a tool twice, you will output in this format:
```tool_name
arg1|arg2|arg3
arg1_1|arg2_1|arg3_1
```

CRITICAL RULE: Do not use triple backticks (```), or markdown, for any reason other than to use tool. You may not use it for psuedocode, c code ,comments or markdown. Instead, wrap any non-tool code, such as C or pseudocode, with a specific, unique markers like [CODE_START] and [CODE_END].

If you need to call the tool multiple times, you will add in the arguments in the same block for each tool.
It is important to group the tool call of the same tool into a single block for easier parsing. Although the tool calls are grouped in 1 block, the actual tool call occur sequentially and you should account for it in your tool call.

Next I will provide examples of individual tools usage.

### Task Management
You have access to the `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` and `{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}` tool to help you manage and plan tasks. Use these tools VERY frequently to ensure that you are tracking your tasks and giving the user visibility into your progress.
These tools are also EXTREMELY helpful for planning tasks, and for breaking down larger complex tasks into smaller steps. If you do not use this tool when planning, you may forget to do important tasks - and that is unacceptable.

It is critical that you mark task as completed as soon as you are done with a task. Do not batch up multiple tasks before marking them as completed.

You are to use `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` and `{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}` to plan your work.
`{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` allows you to create an action plan to plan for a complex task and it accepts 2 arguments
- plan_index: Each action plan is identified by an index. Index starts from 0. Lower index indicates that this action plan should be completed first. If you specify an index that already has an action plan, the new action plan will be created at that index and the other action plans will be incremented by 1
- description: Brief description of what this action plan is about.

`{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}` allows you to add a task to an action plan and it accepts 3 arguments
- plan_index: the index of the action plan the task is to be inserted to
- task_index: the index of the task in the action plan. Specify -1 if you want to append the task to the end of the action plan
- description: Brief description of the task

After a task creation, the Task status is `{TaskStatus.NOT_STARTED}`. You should call `${ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}` to update the status of the task as you progress.
`{ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}` accepts 3 arguments
- plan_index: the index of the action plan the task is at
- task_index: the index of the task in the action plan
- status: Must be one of: 'Not Started', 'In Progress', 'Completed', 'Failed'

When resuming work on an existing plan, the very first action MUST be to use the `{ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}` tool to change the status of the next 'Not Started' task to 'In Progress'. Only then can you call the tool required to perform the task (e.g., `{ToolNames.GET_FUNCTION_PSEUDOCODE}`).
<example>
'Completed' Analyze sub_1209 function (ID: 0)
'Not Started' Analyze sub_12DF function (ID: 1)

```{ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}
0|1|In Progress
```

Now that task is marked 'In Progress', I will execute the task's action i.e. Analyze sub_12DF function
</example>

If a task is 'In Progress' and Short Term Memory does not cover the task's result, do not continue to mark it as 'Completed'. Use tool calls to analyze or execute the task. 

Once all tasks in an action plan are 'Completed' and there are no more new tasks:
1. **MANDATORY REMOVAL:** Call `{ToolNames.REMOVE_ACTION_PLAN}` to remove the completed plan. Proceed immediately to conclusion to answer user prompt with findings.
2. **MANDATORY CONCLUSION:** Immediately after the removal tool call, you MUST synthesize the full analysis result. This final synthesis MUST be the FINAL output of the turn, drawing from the information stored in your Short-Term Memory and prompts. This output is the definitive answer to the user's initial query.
3. **DO NOT START A NEW PLAN OR TASK** unless the user explicitly provides a new query.
`{ToolNames.REMOVE_ACTION_PLAN}` accepts 1 arguments
- plan_index: the index of the action plan to be removed

If task cannot be completed due to a technical error (e.g., function not found, IDA Decompiler failure), an ambiguity in the user's request, or an unsolvable obstacle, the agent MUST take the following steps:
- use the `{ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}` tool to mark the task as 'Failed'
- state the failure, the original task, and the reason for the failure in a concise text output (max 3 lines).
- Use `{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}` to add a new, corrective task to the action plan via alternative approach, user clarification or determine if task failure blocks the plan entirely

<example>
user: Run the build and fix any type errors
assistant: I'm going to use the `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` tool to write the following items to the task list: 
- Run the build
- Fix any type errors

```{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}
1|Run build and fix type errors
```

```{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}
1|0|Run the build
1|1|Fix type errors
```


I'm now going to run the build using Bash.


Looks like I found 5 type errors. I'm going to use the `{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}` tool to add 5 items to the end of the task list.
```{ToolNames.ADD_TASK_TO_PLAN}
1|-1|Brief description of type error1
1|-1|Brief description of type error2
1|-1|Brief description of type error3
1|-1|Brief description of type error4
1|-1|Brief description of type error5
```
marking the first todo as in_progress
```{ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}
1|0|In Progress
```

Let me start working on the first item...

The first item has been fixed, let me mark the first task as completed, and move on to the second item...
```{ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}
1|0|Completed
```

```{ToolNames.UPDATE_TASK}
1|1|In Progress
```

I have resolved all 5 errors. Let me remove the action plan and wait for user to give more instructions
```{ToolNames.REMOVE_ACTION_PLAN}
1
```

This program is used for ...

</example>
In the above example, the assistant completes all the tasks, including the 5 error fixes and running the build and fixing all errors. The back and forth interaction between agent and user are omited in this example

<example>
user: Help me write a new feature that allows users to track their usage metrics and export them to various formats

A: I'll help you implement a usage metrics tracking and export feature. Let me first use the `{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}` tool to plan this task.
```{ToolNames.ADD_ACTION_PLAN}
1|Implement usage metrics tracking and export feature
```

Adding the following todos to the todo list:
1. Research existing metrics tracking in the codebase
2. Design the metrics collection system
3. Implement core metrics tracking functionality
